title: How to Appeal a Health Insurance Denial: A Step-by-Step Guide
excerpt: Insurance companies deny roughly 1 in 7 claims, but most patients never appeal — even though appeals succeed more than 40% of the time. Learn the exact steps to overturn a coverage or medical necessity denial and get the care you've already paid for.
metaDescription: Learn how to appeal a health insurance denial in 2024. Step-by-step guide covering internal appeals, external review, and ACA rights — with sample language.

content.text: A health insurance denial feels like a door slamming shut. You went to the doctor, you followed the rules, and now your insurer says it will not pay. But a denial is not the end — it is the beginning of a process that, when navigated correctly, reverses the outcome in your favor far more often than most patients realize. According to the Kaiser Family Foundation's analysis of ACA marketplace plans, insurers denied roughly 17% of in-network claims in 2021, yet fewer than 0.2% of enrollees filed a formal appeal. Of those who did appeal, about 41% won full or partial reversals at the internal appeal stage alone.

This guide will walk you through every layer of the health insurance denial appeal process — from understanding why your claim was denied, to crafting an internal appeal that speaks the language of medical reviewers, to escalating to an external independent organization if the insurer still refuses. You have federally protected rights under the Affordable Care Act, and this guide shows you exactly how to use them.
Two Types of Insurance Denials — and Why It Matters

Before you write a single word of your appeal, you need to know which category your denial falls into. Insurance companies issue two fundamentally different kinds of denials, and each one requires a different strategy.
Coverage Denials

A coverage denial means the insurer is saying the service, drug, or device is simply not covered under your plan. Common examples include experimental treatments, out-of-network providers when your plan is HMO, or a specific brand-name drug when your formulary only covers generics. Coverage denials are fundamentally contractual disputes — you are arguing about what the plan documents actually say and promise. Your appeal needs to point to the specific language in your Summary of Benefits and Coverage (SBC) or the Evidence of Coverage (EOC) that either includes the service or creates an ambiguity that should be resolved in your favor.
Medical Necessity Denials

A medical necessity denial is more common and, frankly, more winnable. Here the insurer acknowledges the service is covered in principle but claims your specific situation does not meet the clinical criteria for that service. This might happen after an MRI, a surgery, a mental health hospitalization, or an expensive infusion drug. Medical necessity denials live or die on clinical evidence — physician notes, peer-reviewed literature, and the specific clinical guidelines the insurer used to deny you.

content.title: Key Takeaway
content.text: Always request the specific clinical criteria the insurer used to deny your claim. Under ACA regulations (45 CFR 147.136), insurers must provide this information upon request. Without knowing their exact standard, you cannot mount an effective appeal.
Your ACA Appeal Rights: The Legal Foundation

The Affordable Care Act, signed into law in 2010 and upheld by the Supreme Court, fundamentally rewrote the rules for insurance appeals. Before the ACA, denial appeals were largely voluntary processes set by each insurer. Today, if you have non-grandfathered individual or small-group coverage, or coverage through most employer plans, federal law guarantees you specific rights.

items: Right to a full and fair internal appeal reviewed by someone not involved in the original denial
Right to an expedited 72-hour internal appeal for urgent or concurrent care situations
Right to a free external review by an independent organization if the internal appeal fails
Right to all documents, records, and clinical criteria used to deny your claim at no charge
Right to submit evidence and testimony, including a statement from your own physician
Right to continue receiving care during an appeal if your plan covers ongoing treatment

content.text: For employer-sponsored plans governed by ERISA, the regulatory framework overlaps but has important differences. Most large employer plans are 'self-funded,' meaning the employer bears the financial risk and the insurance company is just administering the plan. Self-funded plans are exempt from state insurance laws but must comply with federal ERISA appeal rules, which include written notice requirements and specific timelines. Know which type of plan you have — it affects which regulators you can escalate to later.
Step 1: Read the Denial Letter Carefully

Your Explanation of Benefits (EOB) and the accompanying denial letter contain critical information you need before doing anything else. Look for four specific items: the denial reason code, the clinical guideline or policy number cited, the appeal deadline, and the name and title of the person who made the denial decision.

Appeal deadlines are not suggestions. Under ACA rules, you typically have at least 180 days from the denial notice to file an internal appeal. Missing this deadline can forfeit your right to appeal entirely, so mark the date immediately. For expedited appeals of urgent care, the insurer must decide within 72 hours of receiving your appeal.

If the denial letter is vague — and many are intentionally vague — call the insurer's member services line and ask specifically: 'What clinical coverage determination guidelines did you use to make this decision, and can you send me a copy?' Write down the representative's name, the date and time of the call, and what they said. This call record becomes part of your appeal documentation.
Step 2: Gather Your Evidence

A successful health insurance denial appeal is a documentation exercise. Your goal is to create a paper trail so thorough that the appeal reviewer has no reasonable basis to uphold the denial. The evidence you need depends on your denial type, but these categories apply broadly.
Medical Records and Physician Notes

Request complete medical records from every provider involved in the care at issue. Under the HIPAA Privacy Rule, you have the right to your records within 30 days. Pay particular attention to notes that document your diagnosis, the treatments already tried, why alternative treatments were insufficient, and the clinical rationale for the denied service. These notes are your primary evidence for medical necessity.
A Letter of Medical Necessity

Ask your treating physician to write a detailed Letter of Medical Necessity (LMN) specifically for the appeal. A generic form letter will not cut it. The letter needs to explain your diagnosis using ICD-10 codes, describe the clinical history and what prior treatments were attempted, cite the specific clinical guideline the insurer used, and explain point-by-point why your case meets — or why that guideline is inappropriately applied. A physician who takes 20 minutes to write a thorough LMN gives your appeal vastly better odds.
Peer-Reviewed Medical Literature

Insurance companies base their clinical criteria on guidelines from organizations like the American Medical Association, specialty medical societies, and their own proprietary Milliman Care Guidelines or InterQual criteria. Go to PubMed (free) and find 3–5 recent peer-reviewed studies supporting your treatment. Look especially for guidelines from relevant specialty societies — if your cardiologist ordered an imaging study, the American College of Cardiology's guidelines carry weight. Print the abstracts and highlight the key sentences that support your case.
Step 3: Write the Internal Appeal Letter

Your appeal letter is a business document, not a personal plea. Emotion has its place — briefly — but the body of the letter needs to be clinical, organized, and specific. Here is the structure that works.

items: Opening paragraph: State your name, member ID, claim number, date of service, and the specific denial you are appealing. Say clearly: 'I am writing to formally appeal the denial of [service] on [date], claim number [X].'
Statement of the facts: Describe your medical situation in 2–3 sentences using your diagnosis codes. Keep it clinical and precise.
The insurer's stated reason for denial: Quote it directly from the denial letter. This shows you read it carefully.
Your rebuttal, point by point: For each reason given, address it with evidence. Reference specific page numbers of attached medical records.
Clinical guideline language: If your denial cited specific criteria (e.g., 'patient must fail two prior treatments'), demonstrate that each criterion is met using physician notes and dates.
Supporting literature: Cite your peer-reviewed studies by title and publication. You do not need full citations — just enough to identify them.
Conclusion and request: State clearly what you want — approval of the claim, payment at the contracted rate, or authorization for the service.

content.text: Phrases That Work With Medical Reviewers

Medical reviewers respond to clinical specificity. These phrases signal that you understand the process and have documentation: 'Per the attached physician letter, the clinical criteria outlined in your [guideline name] are fully satisfied as follows...'; 'The treating physician has documented that [alternative treatment] was attempted from [date] to [date] without adequate clinical response, as evidenced by [specific note]...'; 'Current evidence-based clinical guidelines from [specialty society] support this treatment for patients presenting with [diagnosis]...'; 'Denial of this service would constitute a failure of the plan's duty to provide benefits consistent with generally accepted standards of medical practice under 45 CFR 147.136(b)(1).'

content.title: Key Takeaway
content.text: Including the regulatory citation 45 CFR 147.136 in your appeal letter signals that you know your rights under federal law. Reviewers know that non-compliance with ACA appeal rules can expose the insurer to regulatory action — this phrase alone changes the tone of how your appeal is handled.
Step 4: Submit and Track Your Appeal

Submit your appeal by certified mail with return receipt requested, and also through any online portal the insurer provides. You want a paper trail proving when they received it. Keep a copy of everything you send. If you are also submitting by fax, print a confirmation sheet.

Under ACA regulations, the insurer must decide a standard internal appeal within 60 days of receiving it. For urgent care appeals, the deadline is 72 hours. If they miss these deadlines without granting your appeal, you can immediately proceed to external review — the deadline violation itself is grounds for escalation.
Step 5: External Independent Review

If your internal appeal is denied, you have the right to external review. This is a genuinely independent process — the insurer does not choose the reviewer. Under ACA rules, insurers must contract with at least two accredited Independent Review Organizations (IROs). These are clinical organizations staffed by physicians and specialists who review your case with no financial stake in the outcome.

External review decisions are binding on the insurer in most states. That means if the IRO says the claim should be paid, the insurer must pay — they cannot simply override the decision. Success rates at external review vary by type of claim, but for mental health and medical necessity denials, reversal rates frequently exceed 40%. The external review request must typically be filed within 60 days of receiving your internal appeal denial.

Request the external review by sending a written request to the insurer or, in some states, directly to the state insurance commissioner's office. Include copies of all your appeal documentation. The IRO must notify you of its decision within 60 days for standard reviews and within 72 hours for expedited urgent-care situations.
Step 6: State Insurance Commissioner Escalation

If you have a fully-insured plan (most individual marketplace plans and many small employer plans), your state insurance commissioner has regulatory authority over your insurer. Filing a complaint is free and can be done online in every state. The insurer knows that unresolved complaints create compliance risk, and many denials are quietly reversed once a state regulator is involved.

When filing with your state insurance commissioner, provide: your policy number, the insurer's name, a clear description of the denial, copies of your EOB and denial letters, and your appeal documentation. Be specific about what you want — payment of the claim, compliance with ACA appeal timelines, or both. Most states require a response from the insurer within 20–30 days of a complaint.

For ERISA self-funded employer plans, state insurance commissioners have no jurisdiction. Instead, escalate to the U.S. Department of Labor's Employee Benefits Security Administration (EBSA) at 1-866-444-3272. EBSA handles complaints about employer benefit plan violations and can open investigations into plan administrators who systematically violate ERISA appeal rights.
Keeping Records and Building Leverage

Every call you make to the insurer should be logged in a simple spreadsheet: date, time, name of representative, what was discussed, and any reference or confirmation numbers provided. If you are told something verbally that contradicts what is in writing, note the discrepancy and address it in your next correspondence. Insurers are large bureaucracies, and documentation of inconsistent representations can become valuable if you need to escalate to a bad-faith complaint or legal action.

links.text: Know your rights against surprise medical bills under the No Surprises Act
How to dispute a medical bill with your provider
How to write a medical bill appeal letter that gets results

content.text: When to Consider Professional Help

For complex cases — particularly mental health parity violations, experimental treatment denials, or denials involving very large dollar amounts — consider a patient advocate or an attorney who specializes in insurance law. Many work on contingency for significant cases, meaning no upfront cost to you. The Patient Advocate Foundation (patientadvocate.org) provides free case management services for qualifying patients. Additionally, if your denial involves a mental health or substance use disorder service, mention 'Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act' in your appeal — federal parity law requires that mental health benefits be no more restrictive than medical benefits, and violations of this law have produced large judgments against insurers.
Your Complete Appeal Checklist

items: Read the denial letter and identify the denial type (coverage or medical necessity)
Request the specific clinical criteria or policy used to deny the claim
Mark the appeal deadline on your calendar immediately
Request all relevant medical records from your providers
Ask your treating physician to write a specific, detailed Letter of Medical Necessity
Find 3–5 peer-reviewed studies supporting your treatment
Write your appeal letter using clinical language and specific regulatory citations
Submit via certified mail and online portal; keep copies of everything
If denied at internal appeal, immediately request external independent review
If insurer misses statutory timelines, note the violation in all future correspondence
File a complaint with your state insurance commissioner (fully-insured plans) or EBSA (self-funded ERISA plans) if needed

The Bill Cured Blueprint gives you word-for-word scripts, a complete billing error checklist, and negotiation frameworks that have helped patients reduce their out-of-pocket costs by 30–80%. title: How to Read Your Explanation of Benefits (EOB): A Complete Guide
excerpt: Your Explanation of Benefits is one of the most important documents your insurer sends you — and one of the most misunderstood. Learn what every field means, how to spot insurer errors that cost you money, and how to reconcile your EOB against your actual bill.
metaDescription: Learn how to read an Explanation of Benefits (EOB), understand every field, spot overpayment errors, and reconcile your EOB with your medical bill. 2024 guide.

content.text: Most people glance at their Explanation of Benefits, assume it is a bill, and either ignore it or panic. Both reactions are costly mistakes. Your EOB is not a bill — it is a detailed statement from your insurance company showing exactly how it processed a claim, what it agreed to pay, and what portion it says is your responsibility. Understanding how to read your Explanation of Benefits is one of the most financially valuable skills a healthcare consumer can develop, because errors in EOBs — whether they favor the provider or the insurer — are remarkably common and can cost you hundreds or thousands of dollars.

This guide walks through every section of a typical EOB, explains the meaning behind each dollar figure, shows you how the numbers connect to your actual bill, and gives you a practical system for catching the errors that most patients never notice. By the end, reading an Explanation of Benefits will feel less like decoding a government form and more like reading a simple financial statement — which is exactly what it is.
What Is an Explanation of Benefits?

An Explanation of Benefits (EOB) is a document sent by your health insurance company after a medical claim is processed. It is a summary of the financial transaction between your insurer, your healthcare provider, and you. The EOB explains: what the provider charged, what your insurance company agreed was a reasonable amount, how that amount was divided among your insurer and your out-of-pocket obligations, and what (if anything) you still owe.

Critically, the EOB is not a bill. Many patients receive an EOB, see a dollar figure, and pay it — even when no bill from the provider has arrived yet. That can lead to overpaying, underpaying, or paying the wrong party. The provider's bill and the insurer's EOB are two separate documents, and they must be compared against each other before you pay anything.

content.title: Key Takeaway
content.text: Never pay a medical bill before you have received the corresponding EOB. The EOB tells you the maximum amount your insurer says you owe — paying the provider's billed amount before reconciling with your EOB can mean overpaying by hundreds of dollars.
The Anatomy of an EOB: Every Field Explained
EOB layouts vary by insurer, but every EOB contains the same core data fields. Here is what each one means and why it matters.
Billed Amount (or Submitted Charges)

The billed amount is what the provider initially charged for the service — drawn from their internal 'chargemaster' rate list. This number is almost always dramatically inflated. A hospital might bill $8,000 for a procedure that Medicare pays $1,200 for, and that your insurance pays $1,800 for. The billed amount is essentially a fictional starting point that no one — not your insurer, not Medicare, almost never a patient — actually pays at face value. Do not be alarmed by this number, but do make sure it correctly reflects the services you actually received.
Allowed Amount (or Negotiated Rate / Eligible Amount)

The allowed amount is the real number. It is the rate your insurance company has contractually negotiated with an in-network provider for that specific service. If a hospital is in your network, they have agreed to accept this amount as payment in full — no balance billing allowed. The difference between the billed amount and the allowed amount is called the contractual adjustment or write-off, and it simply disappears. You are never responsible for it.

For out-of-network providers, the 'allowed amount' may be calculated differently — often as a percentage of Medicare rates or based on 'usual, customary, and reasonable' (UCR) benchmarks, which are frequently set lower than actual market rates. This is where significant disputes and balance billing can arise. We cover this topic in depth in our guide to out-of-network charges.
Contractual Adjustment (or Plan Discount / Write-Off)

This is the difference between the billed amount and the allowed amount — money that simply disappears from your bill because of the network contract. On a $5,000 billed charge with a $1,500 allowed amount, the contractual adjustment is $3,500. Neither you nor your insurer pays this. If a provider ever tries to bill you for the contractual adjustment portion, that is called balance billing and is prohibited for in-network providers under their contract.
Deductible Applied

Your deductible is the annual amount you must pay out-of-pocket before your insurance company begins paying its share of costs. The 'deductible applied' field on your EOB shows how much of the current claim was counted toward satisfying your deductible. Early in the plan year, most claims apply toward your deductible. Once you have met your full annual deductible, this field should read $0 for the rest of the plan year.

Track your running deductible balance carefully. Your EOB should include a 'year-to-date' deductible summary, but insurers sometimes make errors in this tracking. If a claim applies more to your deductible than it should — perhaps because a prior claim was processed incorrectly — you could overpay. Cross-reference the year-to-date deductible on your EOB against your own records of payments made to providers.
Copayment and Coinsurance

A copayment is a flat dollar amount you owe per visit (e.g., $30 for a specialist visit). Coinsurance is a percentage of the allowed amount you owe after your deductible is met (e.g., 20%). The EOB will show which of these applies and the resulting dollar figure. Note that coinsurance applies to the allowed amount, not the billed amount — a detail that significantly reduces what you actually pay.
What the Plan Paid

This field shows the dollar amount your insurance company actually paid or will pay to the provider. It is calculated as the allowed amount minus your deductible, copayment, and coinsurance. Verify this number against the 'member responsibility' figure: the two should sum exactly to the allowed amount (minus the contractual adjustment). If they do not add up, there is a calculation error somewhere in the EOB.
Member Responsibility (or Patient Responsibility / Amount You May Owe)

This is the bottom line — what the EOB says you owe the provider. It is the sum of any deductible applied, your copayment, and your coinsurance. This number is the ceiling of what you should pay the provider for this claim. If the provider sends you a bill for more than this amount (excluding the contractual adjustment, which you already know you do not owe), that is a billing error or an attempted overcharge.
Reading the Remark Codes and Denial Reason Codes

Most EOBs include short codes in the 'remarks' or 'notes' column that explain how each line item was processed. These codes are standardized — the Claim Adjustment Reason Code (CARC) system and the Remittance Advice Remark Code (RARC) system are used industry-wide. Common examples include CO-45 (contractual adjustment), CO-97 (bundled service), PR-1 (deductible), and PR-2 (coinsurance). If a service was denied or not paid at the rate you expected, the remark code tells you exactly why.

When a line item shows a $0 payment alongside a remark code, it does not automatically mean you owe the full billed charge. It may mean the service was bundled into another procedure code (CO-97), that prior authorization was not obtained (which may be disputable), or that the claim was submitted with an incorrect code. Understanding remark codes is the first step to identifying whether a $0 insurance payment is legitimate or an error you can challenge.
How to Spot Overpayment Errors

Overpayment errors occur when an EOB assigns more to your financial responsibility than it should. These are disturbingly common, and they work in the insurer's favor (or sometimes the provider's) — you pay more than your plan requires. Here are the most frequent patterns.

items: Deductible applied to services that should be exempt: Many plans exempt preventive care services from the deductible. If an annual physical generates an EOB applying charges to your deductible, that may violate ACA rules requiring first-dollar coverage for preventive services.

Wrong in-network vs. out-of-network classification: If you saw an in-network provider but the EOB processed the claim at out-of-network rates — with a higher allowed amount calculation — call immediately. This can add hundreds of dollars to your responsibility.

Coordination of benefits errors: If you have two insurance plans (e.g., covered by both your employer and a spouse's employer), the primary and secondary insurers must coordinate properly. Errors here frequently result in patients being billed amounts that the secondary insurance should have covered.

Year-to-date deductible tracking errors: If your insurer's system has not correctly applied prior payments to your deductible, you may be billed as though you still owe the full deductible when you have already met it.

Coinsurance calculated on billed amount: This is a significant and more common error than most patients realize. Coinsurance should always be calculated on the allowed (negotiated) amount, never on the higher billed amount. If you suspect this error, ask the insurer to show you the calculation in writing.

Out-of-pocket maximum not applied: Once you hit your annual out-of-pocket maximum, the plan should pay 100% of covered services for the rest of the plan year. If an EOB shows member responsibility after you have reached your OOP max, that is a billing error.

content.text: How to Spot Underpayment by Your Insurer

The other direction of error is when your insurer pays less than it should — which, through the magic of balance billing or just provider billing practices, ends up costing you more. Underpayment errors are harder to detect but equally important to catch.

items: Incorrect application of a prior authorization: If prior auth was obtained and the EOB shows a denial for 'no prior authorization,' the insurer may have a system error. Pull your prior auth confirmation number and submit it with a correction request.

Wrong procedure code applied: If the claim was submitted with the wrong CPT (Current Procedural Terminology) code — either by the provider accidentally or as a result of coding — the insurer may have paid at a lower rate than applies to the actual service rendered. Compare the CPT code on the EOB against your medical records.

Bundling when services should be separate: Insurers sometimes bundle (combine under one payment) services that should be separately reimbursable. If your physician performed two distinct procedures in one visit, each with its own CPT code, and the EOB shows only one payment, check whether unbundling is appropriate.

Mental health parity violations: Under the Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act (MHPAEA), mental health and substance use disorder benefits must be no more restrictive than medical/surgical benefits. If your EOB shows lower allowed amounts, higher cost-sharing, or visit limits for mental health services compared to equivalent medical care, that is a potential parity violation.

links.text: How to read your medical bill line by line
Common hospital billing errors and how to catch them
How to dispute a medical bill with your provider or insurer

content.text: The EOB-to-Bill Reconciliation Process

Reconciling your EOB against your provider's bill is the core skill that prevents overpaying. The process is simple once you understand the structure of both documents. Follow these steps every time you receive a medical bill.

items: Wait for the EOB: Never pay a provider bill until you have received the corresponding EOB. You can call your insurer to confirm a claim is still being processed if you are worried about late payment.

Match the dates of service and procedure codes: Every line on your provider bill should have a corresponding line on the EOB. The dates of service and procedure codes (CPT codes) should match. If they do not, one or both documents may have an error.

Compare the 'member responsibility' on the EOB to the 'patient due' on the provider bill: These two numbers should match. If the provider's bill is higher than the EOB's member responsibility, ask the provider to explain the difference in writing.
Check that no contractual adjustment amounts are being billed to you: If the provider is in-network, the difference between the billed amount and the allowed amount should never appear on your bill.
Verify your deductible status: If you believe you have met your deductible but the EOB still applies charges, call the insurer and ask for a year-to-date deductible statement.

Confirm the out-of-pocket maximum: If you have had significant healthcare this year, check whether you have hit your out-of-pocket maximum. If you have, the plan should pay 100% and your EOB should reflect $0 member responsibility.
Document any discrepancies in writing: If something does not match, write to both the provider and the insurer. Keep copies of everything.

content.text: What to Do When the EOB Shows a Denial

An EOB that shows $0 paid by the insurer and a denial remark code is telling you the claim was not paid — but not necessarily that you owe the full amount. If the denial is based on a clinical or coverage reason, you have the right to appeal. If the denial is based on a technical error (wrong code, missing prior auth that was actually obtained, wrong member ID), the provider can simply resubmit the corrected claim.

Your first call should always be to the provider's billing department: 'I received an EOB showing this claim was denied for [reason]. Is this something you can correct and resubmit, or do I need to file an appeal with my insurer?' Providers have billing staff who handle resubmissions daily — many denials are resolved at this level without any formal appeal.
Keeping a Personal EOB Filing System

The patients who successfully catch and correct billing errors are the ones who keep organized records. You do not need an elaborate system — a simple folder (physical or digital) organized by year and provider will do. For each medical service, keep together: the appointment notes or discharge summary, the provider's itemized bill, the EOB from your insurer, and any correspondence about corrections or disputes. When you have these three documents side by side, errors become visible immediately.

Most insurers also offer an online member portal where you can view and download EOBs for all claims in the past 1–3 years. Set up your account and check it after every medical visit, rather than waiting for paper mail. Early detection of errors — before you have paid anything — is far easier to correct than chasing a refund after the fact.

content.title: Take the Next Step With the Bill Cured Blueprint
content.text: Understanding your EOB is the foundation — but turning that knowledge into real savings requires a negotiation strategy. The Bill Cured Blueprint gives you a complete billing error checklist, word-for-word scripts for calling insurance companies and providers, and a proven framework that has helped patients cut their out-of-pocket costs by 30–80%. title: Prior Authorization Denied: How to Appeal and Win
excerpt: A prior authorization denial can block life-changing treatment before it even begins. Learn why prior auth gets denied, how to request an urgent review, and how the peer-to-peer doctor call — the most underused tool in healthcare — can reverse a denial in 24 hours.
metaDescription: Prior authorization denied? Learn how to appeal prior auth decisions, request peer-to-peer review, and use 2024 CMS transparency rules to get treatment approved.

content.text: Prior authorization is one of the most frustrating realities of the American healthcare system. Your doctor decides you need a treatment, a medication, or a procedure — and then an insurance company employee, often without the same specialty training as your physician, decides whether you can have it. According to the American Medical Association's 2023 Prior Authorization Survey, 94% of physicians reported that prior authorization causes delays in patient care, and 33% said prior auth delays led to serious adverse events for their patients. One in four physicians reported that prior authorization has led to a patient's hospitalization.

If your prior authorization has been denied, do not accept it as final. Prior auth denials are appealed and reversed regularly — and there are specific strategies that dramatically improve your odds. This guide covers the full landscape: why denials happen, how to request urgent review, how to use the peer-to-peer physician call (which reverses denials at a remarkable rate), how to file a formal appeal, and what the 2024 CMS final rule on prior authorization transparency means for your rights.
What Prior Authorization Is and Why It Exists

Prior authorization (also called prior auth, PA, or pre-certification) is a requirement that your health insurer approve a treatment, drug, or procedure before it is provided — and before it will be covered. Insurers use prior authorization to control costs and, in theory, ensure clinical appropriateness. In practice, it creates a significant barrier between patients and care.

Prior authorization is most commonly required for specialty medications (especially biologics and specialty drugs that can cost thousands per dose), elective surgical procedures, advanced imaging like MRI or PET scans, mental health and substance use disorder treatments, durable medical equipment, and out-of-network referrals. The specific services requiring prior auth vary by plan, and the list changes from year to year — always confirm with your insurer before scheduling care.
Why Prior Authorization Gets Denied
Understanding why a prior auth was denied is essential to reversing it. The most common reasons fall into these categories.
Wrong or Mismatched Codes

One of the most common — and most easily fixed — reasons for a prior auth denial is a coding mismatch. If the diagnosis code (ICD-10) submitted with the PA request does not match the procedure code (CPT or HCPCS) according to the insurer's coverage criteria, the system may auto-deny before a human reviewer even looks at it. Ask the insurer exactly which codes were submitted and whether there is a code mismatch. If there is, the physician's office can resubmit with corrected codes, often turning a denial into an approval within days.
Wrong Provider or Facility

A prior authorization is typically issued for a specific procedure performed by a specific provider at a specific facility. If any of these details change after the PA is approved, or if the original request listed an incorrect NPI (National Provider Identifier) number, the claim may be denied when submitted. Confirm that the provider listed on the PA exactly matches the rendering provider on the claim.
Step Therapy Requirements

Step therapy — sometimes called 'fail first' — is a protocol where the insurer requires a patient to try a less expensive treatment before approving a more expensive one. If your doctor requested a biologic medication for rheumatoid arthritis but the insurer requires documented failure of two conventional DMARDs first, the prior auth will be denied if that documentation was not included. Step therapy denials are appealable when: the step therapy drug is contraindicated for your patient, you already tried the required drug and it failed, or rapid disease progression makes waiting dangerous. As of 2022, multiple states have enacted step therapy override laws requiring insurers to grant exceptions in these circumstances.
Insufficient Clinical Documentation

Many prior auth denials come down to incomplete documentation. The insurer's clinical criteria may require specific lab values, documented treatment duration, or physician specialty attestations that were not included in the initial request. When this happens, the fix is not an appeal — it is a resubmission with the missing documentation. Call the insurer's prior auth department and ask specifically: 'What clinical information was missing from this request that would have resulted in approval?'
Experimental or Non-Covered Service

If the insurer classifies the requested treatment as experimental or investigational, or if it simply is not a covered benefit under your plan, the denial requires a coverage appeal rather than a clinical documentation resubmission. These cases often require demonstrating that major medical organizations (e.g., the American Cancer Society, the National Comprehensive Cancer Network) consider the treatment standard of care — not experimental.
Step 1: Request an Expedited or Urgent Review

If the delay in care creates a serious risk to your health, you have the right to request an expedited prior authorization review. Under federal regulations, insurers must decide expedited prior auth requests within 72 hours for urgent situations (compared to up to 14 days for standard reviews). For concurrent care situations — where you are already receiving treatment and a denial would interrupt it — expedited timelines apply automatically.

To qualify for expedited review, your physician must certify in writing that applying the standard review timeframe could seriously jeopardize your life, health, or ability to regain maximum function. This certification should accompany the expedited review request. Do not simply call and ask for expedited review — submit a written request with the physician's certification to create a documented record.

content.title: Key Takeaway
content.text: If your insurer exceeds the 72-hour expedited review deadline without a decision, you can treat the delay as a denial and immediately request an external independent review. Under 45 CFR 147.136, missing the statutory deadline is itself a violation of your appeal rights.
Step 2: Request a Peer-to-Peer Review

The peer-to-peer review is the single most effective and least-used tool in the prior authorization appeal process. It is a direct phone call between your treating physician and the insurer's medical reviewer — a doctor-to-doctor conversation about your specific case. Many prior auth denials are reversed during or immediately after a peer-to-peer call, without any formal written appeal.

The mechanism is straightforward: the clinical reviewer at the insurer made a decision based on a paper file. When your actual physician — who knows you, examined you, and has managed your care — explains in clinical detail why the denied treatment is medically necessary for your specific situation, reviewers frequently change their position. A 2021 study in the Journal of the American Medical Association Internal Medicine found that physicians who requested peer-to-peer calls after denials successfully reversed the decision approximately 75% of the time for certain specialty drug requests.
How to Request a Peer-to-Peer Call

Ask your physician's office — specifically the staff member who manages prior authorizations — to request a peer-to-peer review immediately after a denial. The request should go to the insurer's medical management department. Time matters: peer-to-peer calls are typically available only within a specific window after the denial (often 5–14 days). Once that window closes, you may only have the formal written appeal route available.

Prepare your physician for the call: have them review your complete clinical history, the specific denial reason and clinical criteria cited, and relevant published clinical guidelines supporting the treatment. The reviewer will likely ask why less expensive alternatives are not appropriate — your physician should have specific, documented answers. The more specific and clinically grounded the conversation, the better the outcome.
Step 3: File a Formal Written Appeal

If the peer-to-peer call does not resolve the denial, or if your physician's office cannot make the call within the required window, file a formal written appeal. This is your ACA-protected right for non-grandfathered plans. The written appeal should include all of the following.

items: A cover letter stating clearly that you are filing a formal internal appeal of the prior authorization denial, with your member ID, claim or PA reference number, and the date of the denial
A detailed Letter of Medical Necessity from your treating physician that specifically addresses each criterion in the insurer's clinical guidelines
Complete medical records showing your diagnosis, treatment history, prior therapies tried and their outcomes, and why the denied treatment is the appropriate next step
Published clinical guidelines from relevant specialty organizations supporting the requested treatment for your diagnosis
Peer-reviewed studies from PubMed supporting the treatment's efficacy and safety
For step therapy cases: documented evidence of all prior therapies tried, their duration, dosages, and outcomes
For experimental treatment cases: documentation that major medical organizations consider this treatment standard of care

content.text: Step 4: External Independent Review

If your internal appeal is denied, you have the right to external review by an Independent Review Organization (IRO). For prior authorization denials that qualify as adverse benefit determinations (which includes most medical necessity prior auth denials for non-grandfathered plans), external review is required under ACA rules. The IRO decision is binding on the insurer.

Prior authorization external reviews tend to have strong success rates for clinical cases where the treating physician's documentation is thorough. The IRO assigns a reviewer with appropriate specialty expertise — meaning a cardiologist reviews cardiac cases, an oncologist reviews cancer treatment cases, and so on. This is a fundamentally different standard than the initial denial, which may have been made by a reviewer without specialty training.
State Prior Authorization Laws

Prior authorization regulation has been an active area of state legislation in recent years. As of 2024, more than 35 states have enacted prior authorization reform laws covering fully-insured plans. Common provisions include: maximum timelines for standard PA decisions (often 3–5 business days), gold-carding exemptions for physicians with high approval rates (they skip prior auth entirely for services they routinely get approved), continuity of care requirements when a patient switches plans mid-treatment, and step therapy override protections.

For Medicare Advantage plans specifically, CMS finalized a major rule in April 2023 requiring plans to: respond to standard prior auth requests within 7 calendar days, respond to expedited requests within 72 hours, provide specific clinical reasons for denials, and use prior auth criteria consistent with Traditional Medicare coverage policies. This rule took effect for dates of service on or after January 1, 2024.
The 2024 CMS Prior Authorization Transparency Rule

In January 2024, CMS finalized a landmark rule (CMS-0057-F) that significantly strengthens prior authorization transparency and efficiency requirements for payers using federal health exchanges. The rule, which applies to Medicaid managed care plans, CHIP, and QHP issuers in federally facilitated exchanges, requires implementation by 2026–2027. Key provisions include: electronic prior authorization (ePA) capabilities to reduce delays caused by manual fax processes, real-time decisions for items that do not require medical review, a requirement to send prior auth information directly to treating providers electronically, and public reporting of PA approval and denial rates by service type.

The public reporting requirement is particularly significant for patients: it will reveal which insurers are denying prior auths at unusually high rates for specific services, creating accountability that was previously invisible. When this data becomes available, it will provide powerful ammunition for patients and physicians challenging denials at both the individual and regulatory level.

links.text: How to appeal a health insurance claim denial step by step
Your rights under the No Surprises Act for unexpected medical bills
Writing an effective medical bill appeal letter

content.text: When Prior Auth Is Not Required Retroactively

Emergency care is a special case where prior authorization requirements are significantly curtailed. Under both federal law and standard plan documents, you cannot be required to obtain prior authorization before receiving emergency services. If you receive emergency care at an out-of-network facility, your plan must cover the services at in-network cost-sharing levels (with some caveats for post-stabilization care). If your insurer is attempting to deny post-emergency claims for failure to obtain prior auth, this is a violation of federal emergency coverage rules and should be appealed immediately.
Your Complete Prior Authorization Appeal Checklist

items: Read the denial carefully and identify the exact clinical criteria or policy cited
Ask the insurer whether the denial was based on missing documentation or a clinical coverage decision
If documentation is missing: have your physician resubmit with the required clinical information
If a medical necessity decision: immediately request a peer-to-peer review before the window closes
If expedited care: submit a written expedited review request with your physician's urgency certification
Collect medical records, a detailed Letter of Medical Necessity, and published clinical guidelines
File a formal written internal appeal with complete documentation
If internal appeal is denied: request external independent review within the required timeframe
File a state insurance commissioner complaint if your fully-insured plan violates state PA timeline laws
For Medicare Advantage: file with your Medicare plan and escalate to the Medicare Appeals Council if needed

title: Out-of-Network Charges: What You Owe, What You Can Fight, and How to Negotiate
excerpt: Out-of-network billing can result in charges that are 5–10 times higher than what your insurance will cover. Learn the difference between balance billing and standard cost-sharing, when federal law protects you, and how to negotiate OON charges directly — even for care you chose.
metaDescription: Understand out-of-network charges, balance billing, and the No Surprises Act. Learn how to negotiate OON bills and request in-network rate exceptions in 2024.

content.text: Out-of-network charges are one of the most financially destructive surprises in American healthcare. You go to the hospital, follow your insurance card instructions, and then receive a bill for $18,000 because an anesthesiologist or radiologist at that facility happens not to be in your network. Or you knowingly choose an out-of-network specialist and discover that 'covered at 70%' means 70% of an artificially low 'allowed amount' — leaving you responsible for thousands of dollars in balance billing.

The good news: federal law has significantly changed the rules on out-of-network billing since January 2022, and there are concrete strategies for reducing OON charges even in situations where the law does not fully protect you. This guide explains exactly where the protections begin and end, how to negotiate directly with OON providers, and how to ask your insurer for a 'gap exception' — an often-unknown benefit that can dramatically reduce your costs when network access fails.
Two Very Different Problems: Balance Billing vs. OON Cost-Sharing
When people talk about 'out-of-network charges,' they often conflate two distinct problems. Understanding the difference determines which strategy you should use.
Balance Billing

Balance billing occurs when an out-of-network provider bills you for the difference between their full charge and what your insurance paid. For example: an OON surgeon charges $12,000. Your insurer's 'allowed amount' for that procedure is $4,000, and your plan pays 60% of the allowed amount, or $2,400. The surgeon then bills you for the remaining $9,600 — the 'balance.' This practice is what the No Surprises Act was designed to address, and it is now prohibited in many circumstances.
Standard OON Cost-Sharing

Standard out-of-network cost-sharing is different. This is the situation where you knowingly choose to see an OON provider, and your plan covers a portion of the cost — but applies a higher deductible, a higher coinsurance rate, or a lower allowed amount than in-network care. Many PPO plans include OON benefits, but the cost-sharing is intentionally higher to discourage OON utilization. This is not balance billing — it is the plan design you agreed to when you enrolled. Your options here are negotiation with the provider, a gap exception request to the insurer, or a formal appeal if the allowed amount seems unreasonably low.
When the No Surprises Act Protects You

The No Surprises Act (NSA), effective January 1, 2022, is one of the most significant consumer protection laws in healthcare in decades. It prohibits balance billing in specific circumstances that historically generated some of the most devastating surprise bills.

items: Emergency services at any facility: If you receive emergency care, you cannot be balance billed by any provider involved in that emergency care — regardless of whether the facility or any of the individual providers are in your network. You pay only your in-network cost-sharing (deductible, copay, coinsurance) for emergency services.

Non-emergency services at an in-network facility from OON providers: If you have a scheduled procedure at an in-network hospital but an anesthesiologist, radiologist, assistant surgeon, or pathologist at that facility is out of your network, those providers cannot balance bill you. This covers the most common source of surprise bills prior to 2022.
Air ambulance services from OON providers: Balance billing is prohibited for air ambulance services from OON providers who belong to commercial airline fleets.

Provider self-pay acknowledgments: Even when balance billing is permitted, providers must give you advance cost estimates and give you the opportunity to shop for in-network care before non-emergency OON services.

content.text: When the No Surprises Act applies, you owe only your in-network cost-sharing. The provider and your insurer must resolve the payment dispute between themselves through a federal independent dispute resolution (IDR) process — you are removed from the middle. If you receive a balance bill that violates the NSA, file a complaint immediately at the CMS No Surprises Help Desk (1-800-985-3059) or at cms.gov/nosurprises.

content.title: Key Takeaway
content.text: The No Surprises Act covers emergency care and non-emergency care at in-network facilities — but it does NOT cover situations where you knowingly and voluntarily schedule care with an out-of-network provider and sign a written notice of OON status and estimated costs. Keep any notice you sign, because what you were told at signing determines your legal exposure.
When the No Surprises Act Does NOT Protect You
The NSA is not a blanket prohibition on all out-of-network billing. Understanding its limits is as important as knowing its protections.

items: Voluntarily scheduled OON care with proper notice: If you knowingly schedule a non-emergency appointment with an out-of-network provider, and that provider gives you a written notice of their OON status and a good-faith cost estimate before care, and you sign a consent form acknowledging these costs, the provider can balance bill you for the consented amount. You cannot waive these protections for emergency care — only for scheduled non-emergency OON care.

Ground ambulance services: Ground ambulance balance billing is not covered by the NSA at the federal level, though many states have separate protections. This remains one of the most common sources of surprise bills.
Care received outside the United States
Certain grandfathered plans: Some employer plans that have been continuously maintained since before the ACA may be grandfathered and exempt from certain protections.
Indian Health Service and other government programs with separate frameworks

content.text: How to Negotiate OON Charges Directly With the Provider

If you have a legitimate OON charge — either because you voluntarily chose an OON provider or because you are dealing with a ground ambulance or another NSA gap — direct negotiation with the provider is your most powerful tool. Providers know that collecting OON balances from patients is expensive and uncertain. Most will negotiate significantly, especially when you approach them with a concrete offer and a clear understanding of what the market rate actually is.

items: Request an itemized bill: Before negotiating any OON charge, get a fully itemized bill with CPT codes. This lets you research what Medicare and insurance companies typically pay for each procedure — your anchor for negotiation.

Research the Medicare rate: Look up Medicare's payment rates for your specific CPT codes at cms.gov or through tools like FAIR Health Consumer or Healthcare Bluebook. Medicare rates are publicly available and are typically 40–60% lower than billed charges. They are also the most widely accepted benchmark for 'fair' pricing.

Make a specific offer: Do not ask what they will accept. Make a concrete offer. 'I can pay $1,200 for this service today. I've confirmed the Medicare rate for this procedure is $890, and I believe $1,200 is a fair and generous payment that I can make immediately.' Providers prefer certain, immediate payment over uncertain collections.

Request the insurer's contractual rate for similar providers: Ask the OON provider directly: 'What do you typically accept from in-network commercial insurers for this service?' Many providers will tell you. That number is your target.

Offer a prompt-pay discount for immediate payment: Providers typically accept 20–40% less for immediate payment versus a drawn-out payment plan. If you can pay the negotiated amount in full, say so upfront.

Ask about financial hardship programs: Even for OON services, providers often have hardship assistance programs. If the OON balance is genuinely unaffordable, apply for assistance before negotiating — you may qualify for a larger reduction.

Get any agreement in writing before paying: Once you reach an agreement, ask for a written statement confirming the agreed amount constitutes payment in full for the service. Never pay a negotiated settlement without written confirmation.

content.text: Requesting a Gap Exception From Your Insurer

A 'gap exception' — also called a network gap exception or continuity of care request — is a request to your insurance company to treat an out-of-network provider as in-network. This benefit exists in most plans but is rarely advertised or offered proactively. Insurers grant gap exceptions when: no in-network provider with the required specialty is available within your plan's defined access standards, you have an established relationship with an OON provider and switching would cause clinical harm, or the in-network provider wait time is excessive and medically inappropriate.
How to Request a Gap Exception

Call your insurer's member services line and ask: 'I need to see an out-of-network specialist because there are no in-network providers of this specialty in my area. How do I request a gap exception or continuity of care approval?' The representative should direct you to the appropriate department — often the utilization management or case management team.

Your request should document: the specific specialty required, your efforts to find an in-network provider (call logs, names of in-network providers you contacted who had no availability or were not accepting new patients), the clinical urgency of timely treatment, and a recommendation from your primary care physician. If the plan's own provider directory shows no in-network specialists of the required type within the access distance standard (often 30–60 miles or 60 minutes of travel time), that is particularly strong grounds for a gap exception.

Gap exceptions, when granted, are typically issued for a defined period or course of treatment. Get the exception in writing, including the specific in-network cost-sharing rates that will apply. If your gap exception request is denied, appeal it — inadequate network access is a regulatory violation in many states, and the denial itself can be escalated to your state insurance commissioner.

links.text: Your full rights under the No Surprises Act
How to negotiate medical bills when you have no insurance
How to negotiate any medical bill down effectively

content.text: Appealing Unreasonably Low OON Allowed Amounts

Even when balance billing is legitimate, the amount your insurer uses as its 'allowed amount' for OON services may be unreasonably low — leading to higher patient cost-sharing than is justified. Before the No Surprises Act, some insurers used proprietary databases that systematically undervalued OON services. The NSA changed the methodology for calculating OON payments in protected situations, but for voluntary OON care, the old methodologies may still apply.

If you believe your insurer's allowed amount for an OON service is artificially low, you can appeal the determination. Request documentation showing how the allowed amount was calculated — which database was used, what the applicable geographic benchmarks are, and whether the insurer's own in-network rate for similar services is significantly higher. If you can demonstrate that the allowed amount is below both the Medicare rate and the typical in-network rate for equivalent services, you have grounds for an appeal.
Protecting Yourself Before OON Care Happens
The best OON billing situation is the one you prevent. Before scheduling any procedure or specialist visit, take 20 minutes to verify network status and understand your financial exposure.

items: Call your insurer directly — do not rely solely on the online directory, which is frequently out of date
Ask the provider's office to verify they are in-network with your specific plan (not just your insurer's network generally — plan types vary)
Ask specifically about all providers who will be involved: the surgeon AND the anesthesiologist AND the assistant surgeon AND the facility
Request a good-faith cost estimate before scheduling any non-emergency procedure
If the facility is in-network but individual providers may not be, ask the facility to confirm that all attending staff are in-network or that they will use in-network providers only
Know your OON deductible and coinsurance rates, which are separate from in-network cost-sharing on most PPO plans

title: How to File a Health Insurance Complaint — and Actually Get Results
excerpt: Filing a health insurance complaint is free, takes less than an hour, and can unlock leverage that no appeal letter alone provides. Learn exactly where to file based on your plan type — state insurance commissioner, the Department of Labor, or CMS — and what happens next.
metaDescription: Learn how to file a health insurance complaint with your state insurance commissioner, DOL for ERISA plans, or CMS for Medicare. Step-by-step guide for 2025.

content.text: When your health insurance company denies your claim, delays your care, or simply stops responding to your appeals, filing a formal complaint with the appropriate regulator is one of the most powerful tools available to you — and one of the least used. A complaint turns a private billing dispute into a documented regulatory matter that the insurer must respond to, often within 20–30 days. It creates accountability, generates written responses, and frequently produces settlements that internal appeals alone never achieved.

The critical variable is knowing which regulator has authority over your plan. The United States has a fragmented insurance regulatory system: fully-insured plans are regulated at the state level by insurance commissioners, self-funded employer plans are regulated at the federal level under ERISA by the Department of Labor, and Medicare and Medicaid have their own complaint structures through CMS. Filing with the wrong agency wastes time and can delay resolution. This guide walks you through exactly where to file, when to file, and how to write a complaint that gets a substantive response.
When Filing a Complaint Makes Sense

Not every insurance dispute warrants a regulatory complaint. But there are specific situations where a complaint is not just appropriate — it is the right strategic move and may be your most effective path to resolution.

items: Wrongful denial: Your claim was denied on grounds you believe violate your plan documents, federal law (ACA, ERISA, Mental Health Parity Act, No Surprises Act), or state insurance law, and internal appeals have been exhausted or denied.

Bad-faith delays: The insurer is not meeting its statutory response timelines — taking 90 days to respond to an internal appeal that should be resolved in 60 days, or failing to provide required documentation.

Failure to cover mandated benefits: The ACA requires coverage for specific preventive services, mental health parity, and emergency care. If your plan is refusing to cover a federally mandated benefit, that is a regulatory violation.

Balance billing in violation of the No Surprises Act: If you received a balance bill for emergency services or OON services at an in-network facility, and the provider refuses to correct it, a complaint to CMS is appropriate.

Network adequacy failures: If your plan does not have in-network providers of a required specialty within the access distance standards, and the insurer refuses to grant a gap exception, that is a network adequacy violation regulators take seriously.
Failure to send required notices: Insurers must send specific written notices with denials, including appeal rights information. Missing or incomplete notices are regulatory violations.
Coordination of benefits disputes between two plans that have stalled

content.title: Key Takeaway
content.text: Filing a complaint does not prevent you from continuing your internal appeal process. You can — and often should — do both simultaneously. The complaint creates regulatory pressure while your appeal continues through the formal process.
Step 1: Know Your Plan Type

Before filing anything, you need to identify whether your plan is fully-insured or self-funded (self-insured). This single distinction determines which regulator has jurisdiction — and filing with the wrong one delays resolution.
Fully-Insured Plans

A fully-insured plan is one where your employer (or you, if you purchased individual coverage) pays premiums to an insurance company, and the insurance company bears the financial risk of paying claims. Individual marketplace plans, small group employer plans, and many mid-size employer plans are fully insured. These plans are regulated by state insurance commissioners. If you are unsure, look at your insurance card and Summary Plan Description — if it shows a state-licensed insurance company (e.g., UnitedHealthcare Insurance Company of New York, Blue Cross Blue Shield of Illinois), you likely have a fully-insured plan.
Self-Funded ERISA Plans

A self-funded plan is one where your employer bears the financial risk of paying claims — they pay claims directly from company assets, and often hire a third-party administrator (TPA) like Aetna, Cigna, or UnitedHealthcare to process claims. This arrangement is extremely common among large employers. Self-funded plans are governed by the federal Employee Retirement Income Security Act (ERISA), which preempts state insurance law — meaning your state insurance commissioner has no jurisdiction. The U.S. Department of Labor's Employee Benefits Security Administration (EBSA) is the regulator. Your plan documents (the Summary Plan Description) will typically disclose whether the plan is self-funded — look for phrases like 'this plan is self-funded' or 'benefits are paid from the general assets of the employer.'
Medicare and Medicaid

Medicare (including Medicare Advantage) and Medicaid have their own separate appeal and complaint processes managed through the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) and, for Medicaid, state Medicaid agencies. We address these specifically later in this guide.
Filing a Complaint With Your State Insurance Commissioner

Every state has an insurance commissioner (or equivalent) that regulates fully-insured health plans. Filing a complaint is free, can typically be done online in under an hour, and requires the insurer to formally respond — usually within 20–30 days. The commissioner's staff review whether the insurer's actions comply with state and federal law, and can compel the insurer to reconsider their position.
Finding Your State's Insurance Complaint Portal

Find your state insurance department by searching '[State name] department of insurance consumer complaint.' Every state has an online complaint form. The National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) also maintains a central complaint portal at naic.org that can direct you to the right state office. Key states with robust consumer complaint processes include California (Department of Managed Health Care and Department of Insurance), New York (DFS), Texas (TDI), and Florida (OIR).
What to Include in Your State Complaint
A well-organized complaint gets better results than a vague narrative. Include these specific elements.

items: Your name, address, policy number, and insurer name — the regulator needs to identify your account quickly
A clear, factual description of the problem in chronological order: what happened, when, and what the insurer said

The specific law or regulation you believe was violated — e.g., 'The insurer failed to respond to my internal appeal within the 60-day timeframe required by 45 CFR 147.136' or 'The insurer denied a preventive care service in violation of ACA Section 2713'
What outcome you are requesting — specific and concrete: 'I request that the Department order [Insurer] to approve my claim for [service] and reimburse me for the amount I already paid'
Copies (not originals) of all relevant documents: the denial letter, your EOB, your appeal letter, the insurer's response, and any correspondence
A timeline summary if the situation is complex — a bullet-point chronology is easier for reviewers than a wall of text

content.text: Filing a Complaint With the Department of Labor (ERISA Plans)

For self-funded ERISA employer plans, the U.S. Department of Labor's Employee Benefits Security Administration (EBSA) handles complaints. EBSA has regional offices across the country and a toll-free helpline at 1-866-444-3272. You can also file online at dol.gov/agencies/ebsa.

ERISA complaint investigations operate somewhat differently than state insurance complaints. EBSA benefits advisors can contact your plan administrator on your behalf and ask for explanation or correction of plan decisions that may violate ERISA. They do not adjudicate individual claims — they investigate whether plan administrators are complying with ERISA's fiduciary, claims procedure, and benefit rights provisions. However, an EBSA inquiry frequently prompts plan administrators to reconsider decisions, because ERISA violations can expose plan fiduciaries to personal liability.

ERISA also provides a private right of action: if your ERISA plan wrongfully denies benefits, you can sue the plan in federal court for the benefits owed. Before pursuing litigation, exhaust your plan's internal appeals — ERISA requires exhaustion of administrative remedies before a federal lawsuit can proceed. If you believe you have a significant ERISA wrongful denial case, consult an ERISA attorney — many work on contingency for large cases.
Filing a Complaint With CMS for Medicare and Medicaid
Medicare beneficiaries — including those enrolled in Medicare Advantage plans — have multiple complaint channels.
Medicare Advantage

Medicare Advantage plans must comply with CMS regulations, and CMS takes compliance seriously — contract violations can result in plan termination. To file a complaint about a Medicare Advantage plan: call 1-800-MEDICARE (1-800-633-4227), file online at medicare.gov, or contact your State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP), which provides free counseling and can advocate on your behalf. For formal coverage denials in Medicare Advantage, the appeal process runs through the plan, then QIC (Qualified Independent Contractor), then the Office of Medicare Hearings and Appeals (OMHA), and then the Medicare Appeals Council.
Traditional Medicare (Parts A and B)

For Traditional Medicare claim denials, the appeals process runs through the Medicare Administrative Contractor (MAC) that processes your region's claims. The levels are: Redetermination (MAC), Reconsideration (QIC), ALJ Hearing (OMHA), Medicare Appeals Council, and Federal District Court. The SHIP program can provide free assistance navigating these levels.
Medicaid

Medicaid programs are administered by states, so complaint processes vary. Your state's Medicaid agency website will have information about filing grievances and appeals. For managed Medicaid plans (which now cover the majority of Medicaid enrollees), the process is similar to commercial insurance — internal grievance, then state fair hearing. CMS also has oversight authority and accepts complaints about state Medicaid programs that violate federal Medicaid requirements.
What Happens After You File a Complaint
Filing a complaint does not guarantee the outcome you want — but it changes the dynamics of your dispute in several concrete ways. Here is what typically happens.

items: Acknowledgment: The regulator will send you a confirmation that your complaint was received and assigned a case number. Keep this number for all future correspondence.
Insurer notification: The regulator forwards your complaint to the insurer and requires a written response, typically within 20–30 days.

Insurer review and response: The insurer must conduct a review of your complaint and respond in writing. This creates internal pressure — complaints are logged, tracked, and contribute to the insurer's regulatory relationship with the commissioner.
Regulator analysis: The commissioner's staff reviews whether the insurer's response demonstrates compliance with applicable law. If not, they can direct the insurer to correct its position.

Resolution or escalation: If the complaint resolves in your favor, the insurer will contact you with a revised determination. If the commissioner finds no violation, you will receive a written explanation — which, even if not favorable, documents the insurer's stated position for any future legal action.

Potential regulatory action: Patterns of complaints about the same insurer for the same type of violation can trigger broader regulatory investigations, market conduct examinations, and fines. Your individual complaint contributes to this accountability function even if it does not immediately resolve your specific case.

content.text: How Complaints Create Leverage

The practical impact of a regulatory complaint on your individual dispute goes beyond the formal review process. Insurance companies have compliance departments whose job is to manage the insurer's regulatory relationships. A complaint creates a file that must be responded to, reviewed, and closed — and that file is tracked in ways that internal appeals often are not.

Many patients and advocates report that claims which were stuck for months in the internal appeal process were resolved within days of a regulatory complaint being filed. This is not coincidental — compliance teams flag complaints and often have authority to settle them quickly without involving the clinical review team that originally denied the claim. A complaint is, in effect, a signal that you are not going away and that the dispute is now being watched by an external authority.

Additionally, the process of writing a formal complaint forces you to organize your documentation and clearly articulate the legal basis for your position. This improved documentation often makes your simultaneous internal appeal stronger. Regulators and appeal reviewers both respond better to organized, specific, legally grounded arguments than to emotional narratives.

links.text: How to file a formal health insurance denial appeal
Writing a medical bill appeal letter that gets results
How to dispute a medical bill with your provider

content.text: Filing a No Surprises Act Complaint With CMS

The No Surprises Act created a specific federal complaint process for balance billing violations. If you received a surprise bill that violates the Act — a balance bill for emergency services, or a balance bill from an OON provider at an in-network facility — you can file a complaint at cms.gov/nosurprises or by calling the CMS No Surprises Help Desk at 1-800-985-3059.

CMS can investigate complaints, require providers and payers to correct violations, and impose civil monetary penalties. The provider is also required by federal regulation to respond to your complaint. Filing a CMS complaint in a clear NSA violation case is often the fastest path to resolution, as providers who knowingly violated the Act face significant regulatory risk.
Your Complete Complaint Filing Checklist

items: Identify your plan type: fully-insured (state commissioner), self-funded ERISA (DOL/EBSA), Medicare (CMS/1-800-Medicare), or Medicaid (state Medicaid agency)
Exhaust internal appeals first if time permits — regulators generally want to see that you tried to resolve the issue directly
Gather all documentation: EOBs, denial letters, appeal letters, insurer responses, and any written communications
Draft a clear, factual complaint narrative in chronological order with specific regulatory citations
State the specific legal violation — citing the regulation, statute, or plan document provision
State the specific remedy you are requesting
Submit copies (not originals) of all supporting documents
Note the complaint confirmation number and any assigned case number
Continue pursuing internal appeals simultaneously
Follow up with the regulator after 30 days if you have received no update
